result:
I keep thinking about the edge of a photograph—how the image just stops, as if the world beyond the frame no longer exists. When I was small I believed that was literally true: step past the white border and you’d fall into nothing, a pit of unexposed silver halide waiting to swallow your shoe. I used to trim the white strips off with safety scissors and tape them into loops, wearable voids I could slide onto my wrists like the hospital ID band my mother wore the week she disappeared into fluorescent corridors and came back thinner, quieter, smelling of iodine and mashed potatoes.

Memory works the same way: a bright rectangle, then the cut. What lodges isn’t the coherent narrative but the stray sensorial shrapnel. The way the vending machine in the ICU sighed like it was tired of giving up its Doritos. The exact squeak of the orange vinyl chair when my father shifted his weight, preparing to say the thing that would make it impossible for us to look at each other for the next decade. The taste of stainless steel on my tongue after I licked the edge of the bed rail, just to know I was still capable of tasting anything. I have no idea what the doctor actually said—only the hush afterward, the way sound itself seemed to step outside the picture.

Years later I bought a Polaroid camera at a yard sale for three dollars. The seller warned me film was extinct, but I carried the camera anyway, like a respiratory aid for a planet with the wrong atmosphere. One afternoon in March I pointed it at the sky above the grocery-store parking lot and pressed the shutter. The camera ejaculated a square of gray jelly. I slid it into my coat pocket, forgot it, found it again while looking for chapstick. The chemicals had finished their slow argument: the photo showed not sky but a single word written in my own handwriting—RETURN—though I had never opened the print, never touched it with pen. I pinned it to the fridge. Every morning the letters looked more italic, more urgent, as if the syllable were trying to climb out of itself.

I began taking photographs of absences. An empty swing at dusk. The negative space between two lovers on a bus bench. A plate with only the grease shadow of eggs. Each print, when it emerged, carried a word I hadn’t written: LINGER, TENDER, FORGIVE. I accumulated a pocket dictionary of ghost verbs. I slept with them under my pillow; they whispered in the dark like thermostats learning language. One night I woke to find the prints rearranged into a sentence: RETURN TO THE EDGE WHERE THE CUT BEGINS. The paper was warm, as if a heart beat inside the stack. I felt the old vertigo, the sense that the world is mostly margin.

So I drove west until the road surrendered to gravel, then to dirt, then to a trailhead that promised nothing. I walked until the signal bars on my phone flatlined. There, at the lip of a canyon, I lifted the camera and turned it on myself. The shutter stuck; I pried it open with a car key. The photo crawled out like a newborn animal, slick and shivering. I waited the requisite minute, then peeled it apart. No face—only a door, slightly ajar, painted the same color as my childhood kitchen. Through the gap I could see a woman’s hand rinsing a glass under running water. The drain gulped loudly. I recognized the sound: the same throaty swallow our old faucet made the night my mother came home from the hospital, when she poured herself a drink and stood there staring at the tap as if it might explain what portion of her had been subtracted while she was under the tent of anesthesia.

I stepped closer to the edge of the canyon, held the photo at arm’s length. The wind worried its corners. I thought: if I let go, it will either fall into the chasm or fly back toward me, grown larger, a doorway I could walk through. I remembered the loops of white border I wore as bracelets, those portable vacancies. I remembered the pit of unexposed dark. I understood the sentence was not instruction but invitation: to choose the cut, to step past the frame, to risk the nothing that waits beyond the last pixel of color. I let go. The photo hovered, neither ascending nor descending, a bright aberration against the air. Then it folded itself, once, twice, until it was the size of a stamp, and slipped into the crease between two of my fingerprints. I felt it enter the whorls like a seed, cool and possible.

I walked back to the car. The trail seemed shorter, annotated with details I’d missed on the way in: a rusted toy truck half-buried in pine needles, a crow tearing a strip of tape off a speed-limit sign, the smell of wet pennies that always means memory is leaking. When I started the engine the radio came alive mid-sentence: “—and the body remembers what the mind refuses to store.” I drove east, toward whatever comes after the cut, the frame riding beneath my skin like a second, secret skeleton.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
